title: Dermatopharmacokinetics: factors influencing drug clearance from the stratum corneum

records.dose_amount: 500 µl applied via plastic template (diameter 2.5 cm), occluded for 30 minutes

evidence.snippet: Drug application involved a saturated solution (~10 mg/ml) in 75:25 (v/v) propylene glycol/water.

The solution applied (500 µl) was constrained by a plastic template (diameter: 2.5 cm) to prevent spreading and spillage, and was covered with an occlusive bandage during the 30-minute administration period.

A saturated solution of ibuprofen in propylene glycol/water was applied to the forearm of human volunteers for 30 minutes... examination of the clearance phase of a model drug from the SC after a short application of an infinite dose.
Drug application involved a saturated solution (~10 mg/ml) in 75:25 (v/v) propylene glycol/water.
ibuprofen DPK measurements were then conducted on the ventral forearms of the same volunteers.
constrained by a plastic template (diameter: 2.5 cm)

Table II: Quantity of ibuprofen (mean ± SD, n=5) in the SC following a 30-minute application of a saturated drug solution in 75:25 (v/v) propylene glycol/water under different clearance conditions. Delay (h) 0: 13.6 ± 4.1; 0.5: 17.4 ± 0.9; 1: 10.2 ± 1.8; 2: 9.3 ± 1.4; 3: 10.5 ± 4.6; 4: 9.7 ± 2.9; 0.5 (occluded): 7.3 ± 1.0
The integrated amounts in the SC were derived from the areas under the profiles in Figure 3 and yielded a mean (± SD) value of 12.2 (±3.5) µg/cm2.

records.notes: This is an in vivo dermatopharmacokinetic (DPK) tape-strip study, not a classical Franz-cell IVPT/IVRT. No receptor medium or diffusion cell is used. Barrier is human stratum corneum in vivo. Endpoint is SC drug content (µg/cm²) at various time points post-formulation removal (clearance phase). Diffusion area estimated from template diameter of 2.5 cm (π×1.25²≈4.91 cm²). Initial uptake amount at time=0 post-removal from Figure 3 text is 12.2 ± 3.5 µg/cm²; Table II reports 13.6 ± 4.1 for delay=0. Both values are from separate subject sets but same protocol.

extraction_notes: This paper describes an in vivo dermatopharmacokinetic (DPK) tape-strip study, NOT a classical Franz-cell IVPT or IVRT experiment. There is no diffusion cell, no receptor medium, and no permeation flux endpoint. The study measures ibuprofen concentration profiles within the stratum corneum (SC) via sequential tape-stripping, following finite-time topical application of a saturated solution. Only one formulation is used (saturated ibuprofen in 75:25 v/v propylene glycol/water, ~10 mg/ml). The primary endpoints are drug amounts (µg/cm²) in the SC at various time delays post-removal. No receptor medium, synthetic membrane, Franz cell, or flux (Jss) data are present. Cell_type and study_type are set to 'uncertain' as the methodology is strictly in vivo DPK/tape-strip rather than IVPT or IVRT. A single record is returned because only one formulation is tested. The occluded 0.5 h arm is a condition variant of the same formulation, not a separate formulation.

title: Selective modification of skin barrier lipids

evidence.snippet: In vitro experiments using Franz cells, which were selected to follow the SC barrier function capability, demonstrated that Ac-treated skin retained caffeine and ibuprofen on the SC with very low permeation of both compounds into the deeper skin layers.
Ac-treated skin retained caffeine and ibuprofen on the SC with very low permeation of both compounds into the deeper skin layers.
In vitro experiments using Franz cells ... demonstrated that Ac-treated skin retained caffeine and ibuprofen on the SC with very low permeation of both compounds into the deeper skin layers.

records.notes: Abstract only available. Study used Franz cells to assess ibuprofen (and caffeine) permeation through acetone-treated vs. untreated skin. Abstract states 'very low permeation' of ibuprofen into deeper skin layers after acetone treatment, but no numeric endpoints, formulation details, receptor medium, dose, or area are reported in the available content. Full paper is required for quantitative extraction.

extraction_notes: Only an abstract/summary (block 22) is available in the selected content. The abstract mentions Franz cells, ibuprofen, and caffeine permeation experiments with acetone-treated skin, but provides no numeric endpoint values (flux, Q_final, Jss), no formulation composition, no receptor medium details, no dose amounts, no membrane thickness, and no diffusion area. The study compares untreated vs. acetone-treated skin but no quantitative diffusion data can be extracted from the available blocks. A record is created to capture the qualitative study context; confidence is very low.

title: Use of Silicone Membrane Permeation to Assess Thermodynamic Activities of Ionic Liquids and Their Component Cation and Anion

records.dosage_form: ionic liquid (IL) preparation #5 — equimolar lidocaine/ibuprofen IL, neat liquid
records.api_conc_raw: molar fraction of ibuprofen = 0.5 (equimolar with lidocaine); 100% IL, no free ibuprofen

evidence.snippet: Silicone membrane (thickness: 75 µm) was provided by LINTEC Corporation (Tokyo, Japan).
Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4) (approx. 3 mL) was placed on the receiver side
3.0 mL of various IL preparation or lidocaine- or ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution was applied on the donor side
The silicone membrane was cut slightly larger than the effective diffusional area of the horizontal two-chamber diffusion cell.
lidocaine and ibuprofen permeation rates from IL preparation #5 were 3.99 ± 0.06 and 1.18 ± 0.05 µmol/cm2/h, respectively. mean ± S.D., n = 3
fluxss was calculated from the slope of cumulative permeation through the membrane from 1 to 8 h
IL preparation #5 was prepared using equimolar amounts of lidocaine and ibuprofen, it was 100% IL without free lidocaine or ibuprofen
warm water was circulated outside the duplicated cells to keep the applied IL preparation, receiver solution, and silicone membrane at 37°C

records.notes: IL preparation #5 (equimolar lidocaine:ibuprofen = 0.5/0.5). Fluxss calculated from slope of cumulative permeation 1–8 h. Ibuprofen permeation rate from IL #5 = 1.18 ± 0.05 µmol/cm²/h (mean ± S.D., n=3). Diffusion area not explicitly stated. Horizontal two-chamber diffusion cell, 37°C.

evidence.snippet: About three times higher amounts of lidocaine or ibuprofen compared with their solubility in water were added to purified water and stirred at 37°C for 24 h to obtain lidocaine- or ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solutions.
lidocaine and ibuprofen permeation rates from the suspended aqueous solution were 7.92 ± 0.39 and 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm2/h, respectively. mean ± S.D., n = 3
Silicone membrane (thickness: 75 µm) was provided by LINTEC Corporation (Tokyo, Japan).
Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4) (approx. 3 mL) was placed on the receiver side
3.0 mL of various IL preparation or lidocaine- or ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution was applied on the donor side
fluxss was calculated from the slope of cumulative permeation through the membrane from 1 to 8 h

records.notes: Ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution; activity = 1.0 (dQ/dtsol(Av=1)). Permeation rate 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h (mean ± S.D., n=3). Used as reference saturated condition. Fluxss from 1–8 h slope.

extraction_notes: Two clearly distinct ibuprofen formulation/donor conditions are reported with explicit Jss values: (1) equimolar lidocaine–ibuprofen IL preparation (#5, neat ionic liquid, Jss = 1.18 µmol/cm²/h) and (2) ibuprofen-suspended aqueous solution used as the saturated reference (dQ/dtsol(Av=1), Jss = 2.35 µmol/cm²/h). Additional IL preparations (#1–#9) differ only in lidocaine/ibuprofen molar fraction; individual Jss values for each are not tabulated explicitly, only shown graphically in Figs. 3–4 without numeric table data, so they are not extracted as separate records. Diffusion area not explicitly stated in the text. Sink condition maintained by periodic sampling and PBS replenishment. The paper also reports lidocaine permeation data, which is not extracted (non-ibuprofen API).
